pages_preview.text_preview: Pre-Analysis plan for main data collection: Effects of ∗ relative income information † ‡ § Michael Kirchler Kaisa Kotakorpi Satu Mets¨alampi ¶ ‖ ∗∗ Topi Miettinen Xiaogeng Xu Peter Hans Matthews May 12, 2021 Abstract This is the pre-analysis plan of the main data collection (starting on May 10th 2021) of a study of causal effects of relative income information, pre- registered on Open Science Framework website. Data collection for the pilot took place in (December 2020 - February 2020). The pre-analysis plan and a brief data-analysis of the pilot data are found under the same project on the same website. ∗This analysis plan was uploaded and registered on May 12, 2021and not made publicly avail- able. Financial support from the Academcy of Finland and the Yrj¨o Jahsson Foundation gratefully acknowledged. †University of Innsbruck ‡Tampere University §University of Turku ¶Hanken School of Economics, and Helsinki GSE topi.miettinen@hanken.fi ‖Hanken School of Economics, and Helsinki GSE ∗∗Middlebury College, Aalto School of Business, and Helsinki GSE

1 Background The purpose of this pre-analysis plan (PAP) is to pre-register the experimental proto- col and planned analyses of a research project that investigates the effects of relative income information. We plan to write up four distinct projects: an individual welfare project (Project 1), a “just-world” project (Project 2), a policy preference project (Project 3) and a “willingness to act” project (Project 4). Wedividethestructureofthepapersinprimaryhypotheses,secondaryhypotheses and exploratory analysis. 1.1 Primary hypotheses Based on our initial research questions and the results of a pilot study, we focus on the following primary hypotheses: 1. Sense of fairness about income within reference group depends on relative in- come information. 2. Being pleased (vs. disappointed) about relative income is influenced by relative income information. 3. Wage satisfaction is influenced by relative income information. 4. Belief that all have a fair chance at education they seek is influenced by relative incomeinformation,butintheoppositedirectionofindividualwelfaremeasures. 5. Belief that all have a fair chance at the job they seek is influenced by relative incomeinformation,butintheoppositedirectionofindividualwelfaremeasures. 6. Views about the income tax for the top 1% of income earners are influenced by relative income information. 7. Views about job-search-dependent unemployment benefits are influenced by relative income information. 1.2 Secondary hypotheses The following secondary hypothesis will be tested in Project 1: 1

1. The treatment effects of information provision on life and job satisfaction are negligible (Based on pilot results). The next secondary hypotheses will be tested in all papers: 2. For most primary hypotheses, the intervention is externally valid in the sense that information provision reverses some of the effects of misperception. 3. If there is no “surprise” embodied in the information provided, there is no per se treatment effect. 1.3 Exploratory analyses 1. Is the effect of relative income on life satisfaction or job satisfaction a sharp zero (whereas the effect on wage satisfaction and income fairness perceptions are significant)? (Project 1) (As the pilot evidence suggests) 2. Does relative income predict job meaningfulness? (Project 1) (If so, is this a form of motivated belief? If not, can it be used to estimate the differential effects of inequality on jobs with more or less meaningfulness? Remark: this question was added to the survey after the pilot.) 3. Does relative income information predict job search intentions? (Project 1 and Project 4). (Pilot effects are right-signed, but insignificant.) 4. Is there a difference between (political) left and right in beliefs about a just world, both in education, job access and luck - hard work? (Project 2) 5. What is the differential effect of relative income information on humanitarian and economic migration? (Project 3) (Pilot effects were probably right-signed but small. Yet, in the pilot, there were no separate items for humanitarian and work-related immigration. We now expect, for example, that positive rank shocks will be associated with more support for humanitarian immigration.) 6. Does relative income information have no effect on trade policies/globalization? (Project 3) (Pilot effects small, not consistent across distributions.) 7. What is the differential effect of relative income information on political affili- ation? (Project 3) 2

8. Does relative income information have no effect on trust in government, unions and politicians? (Project 3) (Pilot effects were generally small and insignificant across objects of trust and distributions.) 9. Does relative income information have an effect on views about the level of inheritance tax? (Project 3) (Pilot effects small, not consistent across distribu- tions.) 10. Does relative income information have no effect on preferred minimum monthly income? (Project 3) (Pilot effects not consistent across distributions, and sta- tistically insignificant.) 11. Do respondents prefer more equitable distributions in the absence of relative income information? (Project 3) (No evidence from pilot.) 12. How does relative income information influence voluntary tax contributions? (Project 3) 13. How does relative income information influence perceptions on using every means possible to minimize taxes? (Project 3) 14. Howdoesrelativeincomeinformationinfluencevoluntarytaxpayments? (Project 4) 15. How does relative income information influence charitable donations? (Project 4) 16. Are self-assessments of willingness to act (patience, altruism and risk taking) stable in the face of relative income information shocks? (Project 4) (Most treatment effects were consistent across distributions in pilot, but small.) 17. Are self-assessments of social preferences (positive and negative reciprocity, so- cial trust, enjoyment of competition) stable in the face of relative income in- formation shocks? (Project 4) (Most treatment effects were consistent across distributions in pilot, but small.) 18. Do negative surprises about income trigger either (a) increased intentions to gamble, (b) risk-preference, or (c) more purchases of lotto tickets at the end of the survey? (Project 4) 3

19. Does relative income information effect on intentions to buy or sell assets? (Project 4) For all these hypotheses, we will also explore whether effects are different for those with positive and negative misbeliefs, respectively. 1.3.1 Misbelief We will explore the data on misbeliefs to re-evaluate some earlier hypothesis and evaluate some novel ones, the results of which are relevant to all three projects: 1. Is the distribution of misbeliefs centered around zero, overall or by distribution? (Secondary) (Pilot was consistent with other studies on under-estimation.) 2. Consistent with previous studies: are education, age, income and gender sig- nificant predictors of general (all Finland) misbelief? (Secondary) Is the same true of individual distributions? (Exploratory) 3. Are there differences in misbelief by distribution? (Exploratory) 4. Are the beliefs of transfer recipients less accurate? (Exploratory). 1.3.2 Selection and Endogenous Treatment 1. Individuals are more interested to learn their place in the distributions by age, education and occupation than by municipality or overall, with occupation cho- sen most often. (Exploratory) 2. “Usefulness” is a significant rationale for choosing the education and occupation distributions. (Exploratory) 3. For each of our primary hypotheses, we will study how the treatment effect dif- fers when selection of the distribution is endogenous or exogenous. To this end, we will match observations in the endogenous condition, in particular those who choose to learn about a particular reference group, and relevant observations in the control and corresponding exogenous treatments. In matching, we will use register and other variables that are orthogonal to treatment. (Exploratory) 4. Usingthesamematchingmethods, wewillalsoestimatetreatmenteffectsunder selectionforatleastsomeofthosesecondaryhypothesesforwhichthereisstrong evidence of an exogenous treatment effect. 4

1.4 Unregistered Hypotheses Moreover, we will allow for unregistered analyses to be part of the final version of the papers. This will allow for some flexibility of analyses to also identify findings without priors. Importantly, we will indicate the post hoc nature of all exploratory analyses in the papers to signal that they were not part of the primary and secondary hypotheses section of this PAP (Munafo` et al., 2017). 1.5 Pilot We ran a pilot study (pre-registered as approved embargoed registration via Open Science Framework on Feb 19th 2021 with the following objectives:1 1. Assess the response rate and attrition rate for the number of invitees in the main study. 2. Assess effect sizes for sample size calculation for the main study subject to this PAP. 3. Assess the feasibility and technical properties of the survey: test the online survey (question types and answer scales on target population), test sampling strategy and data gathering procedures. 2 Design of the study 2.1 Treatments (with incentives) We implement one control group and six treatment groups, as outlined in Table 1. In the control group, the participants do not see any information about their relative income. In the treatment groups of Age, Municipality, Education, Occupation, and All, the participants see their income rank relative to the respective reference group. In the treatment Choice, the participants make a choice among the five reference groups(thesameage,thesamemunicipality,thesameeducation,thesameoccupation andthewholeFinland)andanswerwhytheymakethechoice. Then, eachparticipant in treatment Choice sees her/his income rank relative to the reference group she/he selected. The relative income rank is defined as which percentage of Finns over 18- years old in the respective reference group who have less disposable income than a 1https://osf.io/42z6d/?view 978a9d01d7c44c18862789b8668cdb86. = 5
